agent1_snippets.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: HVLA Procedure - Lumbar Vertebrae -- Contraindications
agent1_snippets.content: Relative contra-indications would necessitate a discussion with the patient about the possible risks and benefits before the treatment application to inform them of specific risks associated with each condition. Absolute contraindications include osteoporosis, osteomyelitis, fractures in the area of thrusting, boney metastasis, severe rheumatoid arthritis (especially true in cervical HVLA), cauda equina/conus medularis syndrome (or symptoms of numbness/weakness suggestive of these diagnoses), fused spinal joints, osteomyelitis, joint instability, Down syndrome (also especially true in cervical spine manipulation), and patient refusal. Typically, the presence of an absolute contraindication excludes HVLA, as the risks would heavily outweigh any benefit. [6] [4]

agent1_snippets.title: Diskectomy -- Technique or Treatment -- Microlumbar Diskectomy
agent1_snippets.content: In cases of inadequate visualization, removal of the medial aspect of the inferior facet of the superior vertebra may be required. Preserving at least half of the facet joint and 8 to 12 mm of bone from the lateral edge of decompression to the edge of the pars interarticularis is essential to minimize the risk of iatrogenic instability. A laminotomy of the inferior portion of the upper lamina may also be necessary for off-centered disk locations. Following nerve root mobilization with a blunt ball-tipped probe and retraction with a nerve root retractor, disk excision is performed by removing the fragmented or herniated tissue. [13]

agent1_snippets.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: HVLA Procedure - Lumbar Vertebrae -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: This article reviews the background anatomy and physiology behind these techniques as well as discusses the technical aspects of their application. This discussion includes patient screening for contraindications and discussion of alternate techniques to attempt prior to HVLA application. The technical aspects addressed include obtaining a proper osteopathic diagnosis for the vertebral segment in question as well as outlining the set-up and application of the thrusting techniques themselves. Also, common pitfalls and a discussion on improving healthcare team implementation of these techniques are both addressed.

agent1_snippets.title: Interlaminar Epidural Injection -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: The clinical findings of patients who are most likely to benefit from epidural injections are those who have back pain with a radicular component due to herniated nucleus pulposus. [2] Radicular pain in the cervical spine may refer to pain radiating down the upper extremity, while in the lumbar spine classic radicular pain radiates down the lower extremity. Physical exam findings may include those which correspond to radiculopathy, including a positive Spurling test for the cervical spine, or a positive straight-leg-raise for the lumbar spine. [3] [4] Patients with spinal stenosis also may benefit from epidural injections; however, recent studies continue to delineate optimal injectate composition. [5] [6]

agent1_snippets.title: Anterior Inferior Iliac Spine Avulsion -- Pearls and Other Issues
agent1_snippets.content: AIIS avulsion treatment depends on various factors, including fracture severity and the patient's age and activity level. Conservative approaches, such as rest, activity modification, and physical therapy, are typically effective in many cases, facilitating satisfactory outcomes. However, surgical intervention may be warranted in cases of significant fragment displacement or failed conservative management. Recent advancements in surgical techniques, particularly minimally invasive approaches like arthroscopy, have greatly improved outcomes by focusing on reattaching the avulsed fragment and restoring normal anatomy. Despite its potential benefits, surgery carries inherent risks such as heterotopic ossification and nerve damage, emphasizing the importance of careful patient selection and surgical technique to minimize complications and promote optimal recovery.

agent1_snippets.content: Though brief, HVLA techniques on compromised ribs do require powerful forces. It is the responsibility of the physician to reconsider treatment if the patient has the following: Acute rib fracture Pelvic fracture Rib or vertebral cancer Severe low back pain In most cases, the clinician should reconsider HVLA if the patient has the following conditions: Osteomyelitis Osteoporosis Fractures in the HVLA area Bone metastasis Down syndrome Rheumatoid arthritis (severe cases) Pregnancy Acute whiplash Herniated nucleus pulposus Postoperative conditions Vertebral artery ischemia History of hemophilia or ongoing anticoagulation therapy

agent1_snippets.title: Intradiscal Electrothermal Therapy -- Technique or Treatment
agent1_snippets.content: Before the IDET procedure, the discs undergo selection on clinical grounds according to the level of a provocative discography. This procedure takes place under biplanar fluoroscopy or C-arm fluoroscopy in which a single needle approach is performed with a stylet needle that ranges in size from 18- to 22- gauge. A posterolateral extradural approach is preferred to avoid damage to the dural sac. Once the lumbar level is confirmed and there is corroboration of the painful disc by provocative discography, the IDET procedure can begin.

agent1_snippets.content: The hands positioned on the sacral bone must not slide on the tissue, but must induce harmonious movements. The manual approach ends when the practitioner judges the tissue under the hands or the tissues under the sacral bone to have shown improvement. This technique can have other goals. For example, improve the relationship between the base of the sacrum and L5, and consequently, positively influence craniosacral mobility. The sacroiliac joint is innervated and can give painful symptoms; this approach could improve the articular relationship of these joints and reduce nociceptive afference.

agent1_snippets.content: The degree of slippage is best characterized on the lateral x-ray.  An oblique view x-ray of the lumbar spine is a good tool to assess the pars defect.  CT Scan of the lumbar spine is the definitive study to assess the pars defect. MRI is the study of choice for evaluation of soft tissue structures including intervertebral discs, ligamentum flavum, the thecal sac, nerve roots, and the conus medullaris in the lumbar spine. MRI is needed for patients who fail conservative treatments and are planning a surgical intervention.

agent1_snippets.title: Epidural Steroid Injections -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Another common indication for epidural steroid injection is nerve root irritation secondary to spinal stenosis. Spinal stenosis is a condition causing the narrowing of the spinal canal or the foramina of exiting nerve roots. This narrowing is most commonly caused by arthritis of the vertebral joints (facets) or intervertebral disc pathology, as discussed above. Other conditions where epidural steroid injections may be indicated include: Nonspecific radiculitis Vertebral bone spurs impinging spinal nerve roots Thickening of ligamentum flavum Postlaminectomy syndrome Facet or nerve root cyst with radicular pain Postherpetic or posttraumatic (including intercostal) neuralgia Compression fracture with radicular pain Spondylolysis Spondylolisthesis Scoliosis causing nerve root irritation

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
agent2_snippets.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions that target the femur and adjacent structures. Child procedures include epiphyseal arrest techniques—“Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)”—which modulate growth plates to correct deformities. Fracture‑stabilizing operations encompass “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur” (with and without fracture reduction), “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur” (with or without internal fixation), and “Trochanterplasty.” Osteoplastic modifications are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” allowing alteration of femoral length and contour. Preventive and corrective measures include “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur.” Prosthetic management is covered by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Clinically, these procedures address the primary anatomical site of lower‑limb fractures, exemplified by the entity “fracture of lower limb” classified as an unspecified closed fracture of the femoral neck, underscoring the femur’s central role in diagnosis, fracture stabilization, growth modulation, length alteration, and implant revision within orthopedic care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431
agent2_snippets.content: Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is classified as a treatment modality for several specific orthopedic injuries. It is considered a type of treatment for fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur, for fractures or dislocations of the lower extremity excluding the hip and femur, and for fractures or dislocations of the radius and ulna. In each of these contexts, the procedure involves surgically stabilizing the bone without performing a reduction of the fracture, thereby providing a consistent therapeutic approach across multiple anatomical sites.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 755
agent2_snippets.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” is a subcategory of “treatment; fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur)” and encompasses a range of operative interventions focused on the femur, proximal tibia, fibula, and trochanteric region. Specific procedures classified as children of this category include “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” which addresses growth‑plate modulation of the distal femur, and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus),” which corrects angular deformities of the lower limb by partially arresting growth. The category also contains “Internal fixation of femur” and its variant “Internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction,” both of which provide stable fixation for femoral fractures, with the latter used when anatomical reduction is not achieved. Bone‑shaping and lengthening techniques are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” and the more complex “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” which allow for correction of limb length discrepancies and deformities through controlled osteotomy and segment manipulation. Management of post‑traumatic deformity is addressed by “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” which restores proper alignment of the femur distal to the proximal femoral region. Additionally, “Trochanterplasty” is listed as a child procedure, indicating surgical modification of the greater trochanter for indications such as hip abductor insufficiency or prosthetic fitting. Collectively, these procedures illustrate the comprehensive surgical toolkit available for addressing fractures, dislocations, growth‑plate disorders, angular deformities, malunions, and length discrepancies in the thigh and leg, thereby supporting optimal functional recovery and limb alignment.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 164
agent2_snippets.content: The “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” category aggregates a wide array of operative interventions that target the femur, tibia, fibula, hip, and associated joint structures. Direct femoral procedures include internal fixation of the femur (with or without fracture reduction), intramedullary nailing, open reduction of femur fractures (with or without internal fixation), suture of the periosteum of the femur, repair of distal femur malunion, femoral‑head reconstruction (إعاضة رأس الفخذ), and femoral shortening performed with a blade plate. Osteoplastic techniques—osteoplasty of the femur, femoral lengthening, shortening, and combined lengthening‑shortening with segment transfer—address limb‑length discrepancies and deformities. Growth‑modulating strategies comprise epiphysiodesis of the distal femur (and proximal tibia or fibula) and hemiepiphyseal arrest of the distal femur, proximal tibia, or fibula for correction of genu varus or valgus. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1765
agent2_snippets.content: Craterization of bone and sequestrectomy of tarsals or metatarsals are both categorized as other or therapeutic procedures on bone, indicating that they are surgical interventions involving bone tissue. Craterization of bone refers to a technique that creates a crater‑like defect in bone for therapeutic purposes, while sequestrectomy of tarsals or metatarsals involves the removal of necrotic bone fragments from the foot’s tarsal or metatarsal bones. Both procedures are employed in orthopedic and surgical practice to treat bone pathology and promote healing.

agent2_snippets.content: and radius/ulna injuries; a specific instance is internal fixation of the clavicle without fracture reduction. All clavicular and shoulder‑arm procedures—including osteotomy, osteoplasty, periosteal suture, and various open‑reduction and internal‑fixation techniques—are organized under the “SHOULDER AND ARM: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” hierarchy, which itself is positioned as a sub‑category of the broader treatment domain for fracture or dislocation of the radius and ulna. The Standardized MedDRA Query for osteoporosis/low bone mass (SMQ) incorporates entities such as hip‑head prosthesis (إِعَاضَة رَأْس الفَخِذ) and multiple fractures (كسور متعددة), linking these conditions to the same osteoporotic spectrum that informs clinical reporting and patient‑care decisions.
